source: Vivian Gornick, The Situation and the Story: situation is what happened; story is what the writer makes of it
article: When I was fourteen, my grandfather got sick and I had to help take care of him. It was hard, but I learned a lot about responsibility and what it means to be there for family.

critiques.text_quote: When I was fourteen, my grandfather got sick and I had to help take care of him. It was hard, but I learned a lot about responsibility and what it means to be there for family.
critiques.question: Gornick's distinction: this is the situation (he got sick, you helped), not the story (what the experience made you, what you came to see, what changed inside you). The reader has the bare facts and a stated lesson. The story is the part you have not written yet, the part where the situation becomes meaning.
critiques.why_it_matters: Without the story, the essay reports an experience without revealing the writer; the reader has nothing to carry away.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Pick one specific moment from those months (the first night he didn't recognize you, the time he asked for water and you didn't hear him) and write it as a scene. Let the moment do the work the summary tried to do.

source: Anne Lamott, Bird by Bird: show, do not tell
article: I felt overwhelmed by everything that was happening. The pressure of school, my parents' divorce, and my friend's accident all hit me at once. It was the hardest year of my life.

critiques.text_quote: I felt overwhelmed by everything that was happening.
critiques.question: 'I felt overwhelmed' is the writer telling the reader what to feel. Lamott's old rule: show the reader the four small concrete details that let them feel it themselves. The cold cereal you ate standing up because you had stopped sitting down to eat. The unread texts piling up. The walk to school where you took a longer route to delay arrival by six minutes.
critiques.why_it_matters: When the writer tells the reader what to feel, the reader feels nothing; specifics earn the emotion.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Cut 'I felt overwhelmed' and write three specific small details from a particular day that week. Let the reader assemble the feeling.

source: Mary Karr, The Art of Memoir: the earned lesson
article: Through this experience, I learned that family is what truly matters in life. I will carry this lesson with me forever.

critiques.text_quote: Through this experience, I learned that family is what truly matters in life. I will carry this lesson with me forever.
critiques.question: The lesson is handed to the reader rather than earned through the writing. Karr's standard for memoir: if the reader has to take your word that the lesson follows from the experience, the writing has not done its job. The story has to make the lesson inevitable, and then the lesson does not even need to be stated.
critiques.why_it_matters: Stated lessons in personal essays read as the writer reaching past the experience to package it; readers feel managed.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Cut the explicit lesson. Trust the scenes to carry it. If you must state something at the end, name what you are still not sure of, not what you concluded.

source: Phillip Lopate, on the wrong moment
article: I will never forget the day of the championship game. I scored the winning goal in overtime and our team won the state title. The crowd went wild and I felt like a hero.

critiques.text_quote: I will never forget the day of the championship game. I scored the winning goal in overtime and our team won the state title. The crowd went wild and I felt like a hero.
critiques.question: You picked the moment that looks largest from outside. Personal essays work better on smaller moments that reveal more. The walk back to the bench between periods. The look your teammate gave you during the third penalty. The text from your mom that you read in the parking lot afterward. The dramatic moment is rarely the revealing one.
critiques.why_it_matters: The big-public moment is what every other essay picks; smaller specific moments are what readers carry away.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Try the same essay built around a smaller moment from that season: a practice, a loss, the day you almost quit. The championship can sit in the background, doing less work than the small moment does.

article: My greatest weakness is that I care too much. I work too hard, sometimes staying up until two in the morning to finish a project, because I cannot accept anything less than my best.

critiques.text_quote: My greatest weakness is that I care too much. I work too hard, sometimes staying up until two in the morning to finish a project, because I cannot accept anything less than my best.
critiques.question: 'I care too much' is the classic false vulnerability: a weakness that is really self-praise. Real vulnerability has a cost. A college-admissions reader has seen this sentence five hundred times this season, and the move is now invisible to them.
critiques.why_it_matters: False vulnerability signals to the reader that the writer is performing, not revealing.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Try a real weakness, the kind that has a cost: the friendship you lost because you were rigid about being right, the class you avoided because you were afraid you'd fail. Then show what you did with it.

article: It was during my freshman year that I undertook a transformative journey of personal growth and self-discovery, which profoundly impacted my worldview and shaped the person I have become today.

critiques.text_quote: It was during my freshman year that I undertook a transformative journey of personal growth and self-discovery, which profoundly impacted my worldview and shaped the person I have become today.
critiques.question: Karr's standard: voice is the one thing nobody else can imitate, the proof you wrote this. 'Transformative journey of personal growth and self-discovery' is the common college-essay register, the voice every admissions reader has read a thousand times. Your actual voice is somewhere underneath this; the formal register has buried it.
critiques.why_it_matters: When the voice flattens into the genre's default, the essay disappears into the pile.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Rewrite the sentence in the voice you would use to tell a close friend the same thing. Strip the abstractions ('transformative,' 'self-discovery,' 'worldview'). Replace with the specific year-old thing that started the change.

source: Vivian Gornick, on the everyone essay
article: Losing my grandmother was the hardest thing I have ever been through. She had always been there for me, and her death taught me to appreciate the time I have with the people I love.

critiques.text_quote: Losing my grandmother was the hardest thing I have ever been through. She had always been there for me, and her death taught me to appreciate the time I have with the people I love.
critiques.question: This essay could be written by any of fifty thousand applicants this year. Gornick: the personal essay works when the specifics are nobody else's. What was the specific thing your grandmother said, the specific kitchen, the specific Saturday-morning ritual? Without specifics that could only be yours, the essay is a category, not a story.
critiques.why_it_matters: Generic loss-of-grandparent essays compete with thousands of others; the reader cannot distinguish you from the form.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Replace 'always been there for me' with the specific Saturday at her kitchen table. The specifics are what convert a category into a person.

source: Standard writing-workshop diagnostic: reflection at the wrong altitude
article: The accident changed my perspective on life. I now understand the importance of resilience and the value of cherishing each moment we are given.

critiques.text_quote: I now understand the importance of resilience and the value of cherishing each moment we are given.
critiques.question: The reflection stays at the level of pull-quote abstractions: 'resilience,' 'cherishing each moment.' The scene preceding it deserves a sharper second beat. Gornick: the reflection has to do the second job, not paraphrase the first. What did the accident actually change in how you make decisions, what specific habit, what you noticed about your father afterward?
critiques.why_it_matters: Surface reflection wastes the work the scene did; the reader was promised more.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Push one altitude lower. Not 'I learned resilience'; rather, 'I learned my father will not say he is afraid, and that I have to read his shoulders to know.' Specific, not abstract.

source: Standard college-essay diagnostic: the unearned twist
article: She seemed cold and distant the entire week of the camp. I tried to talk to her but she would only give one-word answers. Then on the last day, she handed me a letter saying I had changed her life.

critiques.text_quote: Then on the last day, she handed me a letter saying I had changed her life.
critiques.question: The turn arrives without the setup that would let the reader believe it. The week's interactions, as you wrote them, do not give the reader any reason to expect this revelation, and the essay does not show how it landed for you. The reader feels handed an ending the essay has not earned.
critiques.why_it_matters: An unearned twist makes the reader trust the writer less, not more.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Either earn the turn (one specific moment during the week where you saw something the cold front concealed), or change the structure so the letter is the beginning, not the end, and the essay reflects on what you missed.

source: Vivian Gornick, on situation vs. story: the small specific moment
article: I remember sitting at the kitchen table the morning after my parents told us about the divorce. My mother was making pancakes the way she always did on Saturday, and I realized that the small ritual was the only thing that was still going to be the same.

critiques.text_quote: I remember sitting at the kitchen table the morning after my parents told us about the divorce. My mother was making pancakes the way she always did on Saturday, and I realized that the small ritual was the only thing that was still going to be the same.
critiques.issue_label: scene works, lesson could trust itself
critiques.question: The scene itself does almost all the work the essay needs: the pancakes, the Saturday, the realization that follows from the specific. The line 'the only thing that was still going to be the same' is doing too much explaining. The reader was already there.
critiques.why_it_matters: Even good essays sometimes over-explain after a scene that has already landed; trust the reader more.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Try cutting the explanatory clause. End on 'My mother was making pancakes the way she always did on Saturday.' The reader will arrive at the realization on their own, which makes it more theirs.
critiques.replacement: I remember sitting at the kitchen table the morning after my parents told us about the divorce. My mother was making pancakes the way she always did on Saturday.
